question: What specific demands did Tyson workers present in their petition for improved conditions, and how did Tyson Foods respond to these demands?
answer: Tyson workers demanded a $4 per hour wage increase for all workers, no restrictions on bathroom or drinking water breaks, more frequent short breaks, and paid sick leave. Tyson Foods claimed it had 'no reason to deny breaks' and stated the petition was 'misrepresented' to workers.

video: Workers at a Tyson plant are demanding a $4/hour increase for all workers across the board, no restrictions on bathroom or drinking water breaks, and more frequent short breaks.
A group of people wearing masks gathered to carry petitions wrapped in trays towards Tyson's facility, motivated by concerns over worker safety and rights.
text: Worker advocacy group Venceremos delivered a petition to Tyson Foods demanding a $4/hour wage increase, unrestricted bathroom/water breaks, more frequent short breaks, and paid sick leave.
Tyson Foods claimed it has 'no reason to deny breaks' and stated the petition was 'misrepresented' to workers.

question: What were the rates of carpal tunnel syndrome and nerve disorders among poultry workers at standard line speeds, and how did a survey of Alabama poultry workers' injury reports compare to government data?
answer: At standard line speeds, 34% of poultry workers had carpal tunnel syndrome and 76% had nerve disorders. In a survey, 72% of Alabama poultry workers reported significant work-related injury or illness, contrasting with the government data rate of 4.5% for the same year.

video: Thirty-four percent of participants had evidence of carpal tunnel syndrome based on a specific case definition.
Seventy-six percent of participants had an abnormal nerve conduction test result in at least one hand.

The Southern Poverty Law Center and Alabama Appleseed reported in 2013 that 72% of respondents described suffering significant work-related injury or illness while working in the poultry processing industry.
The Bureau of Labor Statistics data shows poultry processing has an incidence rate of nonfatal occupational injuries and illnesses at 4.5 cases per 100 full-time workers in 2013.
text: Poultry workers suffer high rates of repetitive motion injuries, including a 34% rate of carpal tunnel syndrome and a 76% rate of nerve disorders at standard line speeds.
A survey of Alabama poultry workers found 72% reported significant work-related injury or illness, contrasting with a 4.5% rate in government data for the same year.
